ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific entry for Sotong Masak Hitam (braised squid in black ink). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked squid/cuttlefish products; no specific code for the Malay black-ink preparation. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sotong Masak Hitam is approximately 200 g, representing a typical rice-accompaniment portion as served in Malay home cooking or hawker settings.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Squid / Seafood Dishes; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175168, Squid, mixed species, cooked) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD seafood entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry for Sotong Masak Hitam as a complete dish. Values adapted from HPB Singapore cooked squid entries and USDA FDC cooked squid (FDC ID 175168), adjusted for the braised preparation with aromatics, tamarind, and squid ink. Sodium elevated relative to plain cooked squid to reflect added salt and natural ink sodium. Saturated fat estimated from squid lipid profile and cooking oil contribution. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json data (confidence 0.90–0.95) and are retained as the best available approximation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Analogous estimate: high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dish with acidic tamarind component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sotong Masak Hitam. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high protein content (14.5 g/100g) which blunts glycemic response; (2) very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g); (3) presence of tamarind acidity which further reduces postprandial glucose response; (4) comparable to other high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 3.5 × 2) / 100 = 2.1 g, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g (640 mg per standard 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced added salt during preparation, as the 320 mg sodium per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target; the potassium content (280 mg/100g) partially offsets sodium load and supports vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g) and estimated low GI of 30
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sotong Masak Hitam is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g), high-quality protein (14.5 g/100g), and estimated low glycemic index of 30; the primary glycemic concern is the rice or bread typically served alongside, not the dish itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Squid cholesterol content (approximately 185 mg/100g) and sodium level
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While squid provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the relatively high cholesterol content (~185 mg/100g) and sodium (320 mg/100g); moderate portions of 100–150 g are advisable, and preparation with minimal added oil and salt is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from squid and anti-inflammatory aromatics (ginger, lemongrass)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Squid provides modest omega-3 fatty acids and the aromatic base of ginger and lemongrass has documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, individuals with shellfish or mollusk sensitivities should exercise caution as squid may trigger flares in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sotong Masak Hitam (Squid in Black Ink) is a classic Malay and Indonesian seafood dish distinguished by its jet-black gravy derived from the natural ink of the squid released during braising. The dish is prepared by cooking whole or sliced squid in a spice paste of shallots, garlic, ginger, and lemongrass, with tamarind (asam jawa or asam keping) added for tanginess and acidity. The squid ink thickens the sauce and imparts a deep, savory umami character. Despite its dramatic appearance, the flavor profile is bright, slightly sweet, and well-balanced. Texturally, the squid is tender when properly cooked and the gravy is thick and coating. Nutritionally, the dish is high in quality protein (14.5 g/100g), moderate in fat (4.8 g/100g), and very low in carbohydrates (3.5 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 30), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate-to-high at 320 mg/100g, which is a consideration for hypertension management. Squid is a notable source of vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100g), selenium (35 mcg/100g), phosphorus (180 mg/100g), and potassium (280 mg/100g). The dish is a staple rice accompaniment in Malay home cooking, coastal Malaysian and Singaporean hawker culture, and Indonesian cuisine, where it is known as Cumi Masak Hitam. It is typically served at lunch or dinner alongside steamed white rice.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Sotong Masak Hitam as a complete prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore cooked seafood entries and USDA FDC cooked squid (FDC ID 175168), adjusted for the braised preparation with aromatics, tamarind, and squid ink. The input enriched_json values (confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with these approved-source analogues and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No exact approved-source entry for Sotong Masak Hitam as a complete dish. Values adapted from HPB Singapore cooked squid/seafood entries and USDA FDC cooked squid (FDC ID 175168, mixed species, cooked), adjusted for braised preparation with aromatics, tamarind, and squid ink. Saturated fat (1.2 g), monounsaturated fat (1.8 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g), and cholesterol (185 mg) estimated from squid lipid profile per USDA FDC and cooking oil contribution. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: water_g is set to -1 as detailed moisture data for this specific braised preparation is not available in HPB Singapore or USDA FDC databases as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as squid and the aromatics used contain negligible industrially-produced trans fats; this is confirmed negligible per USDA FDC squid entries. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 as squid contains negligible vitamin D per USDA FDC (FDC ID 175168 reports 0 mcg vitamin D). 3. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 3.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.1 g (very low). 4. CHOLESTEROL NOTE: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (~185 mg/100g per USDA FDC); individuals on strictly low-cholesterol diets should limit consumption. 5. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: Tamil and Hindi names are transliterations/descriptive translations as no standardized culinary Tamil or Hindi term for this specific Malay dish is established in approved sources. Chinese names (黑墨鱿鱼 / 黑墨魷魚) are descriptive translations used in Singapore Chinese-language food contexts. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for this specific Malay preparation.
